title: Royal icing — flat coat
subtitle: Hard-setting white icing for coating fruitcakes
excerpt: Classic royal icing mixed to a spreadable consistency and applied in layers to produce the smooth, flat, hard white surface traditional on Christmas and celebration cakes. Each layer is left to dry before the next is applied.
sourceNotes: Royal icing appears in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, as the standard icing for celebration and Christmas cakes. The technique of building up flat coats, allowing each to dry, is documented in Victorian and Edwardian confectionery guides. The addition of a small amount of glycerine to prevent the icing setting to rock-hard is a later 20th century refinement documented in British sugarcraft publications.

content.text: Royal icing is a hard-setting icing made from icing sugar and egg white. For flat coating a fruitcake it is applied in two or three thin layers, each allowed to dry before the next is added. The
sits between the stiff icing used for piped decorations and the loose flood icing used to fill in shapes. A small amount of glycerine softens the set so the iced cake can be cut without shattering.

Beat the egg whites with the lemon juice until foamy. Gradually sift in the icing sugar, beating well after each addition, until the icing is white, smooth, and thick. Add the glycerine if using. Check the consistency: lift the beater; the icing should form a peak that slowly bends over. Add water a teaspoon at a time if too stiff, or extra sifted icing sugar if too soft. Keep covered with cling film pressed directly onto the surface to prevent crusting.

Place the cake on a board. Apply a generous amount of icing to the top of the cake using a palette knife. Use the palette knife held at a low angle and drawn across the surface in one sweep to level the icing. Apply icing to the sides, drawing the palette knife around the cake at a consistent angle to build up an even coat. Smooth over any ridges. Leave to dry uncovered for at least 8 hours before applying a second coat.

Lightly sand down any ridges on the first coat with fine sandpaper or a dry palette knife before applying the second coat in the same way. Two coats are usually sufficient for a clean result; a third coat is used for wedding and exhibition cakes where a perfectly smooth surface is required.

recipe.yieldDescription: enough for a 20 cm cake, 2 to 3 coats
